questions.scenario: The cluster has critical system pods and normal application pods. Which node pool habit matches the article?

options.text: Use a system node pool for critical system pods and user node pools for application workloads.
Put every pod in the default namespace so scheduling is simpler.
Run all pods on one tiny node because Kubernetes will ignore capacity.
Avoid node pools because AKS does not use worker VMs.

questions.explanation: AKS uses node pools of worker VMs. System and user pools help separate cluster-critical pods from application capacity.

questions.scenario: Which reasons can justify a separate user node pool?

options.text: A memory-heavy service needs a different VM size family.
A sensitive workload needs isolation through labels, taints, or policy.
A pod needs a prettier name.

questions.explanation: Node pools are capacity and isolation boundaries. Naming alone is not a reason to create a new pool.

questions.scenario: A team asks why an AKS cluster still has compute cost when few requests arrive. Which answer is best?

options.text: The node pools are Azure VMs, and their capacity exists even when application traffic is low.
Pods are billed as separate physical servers.
Ingress controllers create paid Azure subscriptions.
Kubernetes services store all application data by default.

questions.explanation: AKS worker nodes are Azure VMs. The team needs to reason about node pool capacity, autoscaling, and idle cost.
